text: Feline immunodeficiency virus (FIV) and feline leukaemia virus (FeLV) are two of the major retroviral diseases affecting felines worldwide and can lead to immunosuppression, disease secondary to opportunistic infections, and a reduced lifespan. There has been no study done to date regarding the prevalence of FIV/FeLV in shelter cats in Portugal. Cats in shelters often have unknown medical history and can lead precarious lifestyles, thus representing a high-risk population for the exposure and transmission of FIV/FeLV infections. The aim of the present study was to assess retrospectively the prevalence of FIV/FeLV among a population of 326 stray cats admitted in a official shelter in the north-eastern Portugal over a period of six years (2017–2023). Blood samples were obtained during the intake examinations, and retroviral infections were detected by point-of-care immunochromatographic test (antibody detection). Overall, the FIV/FeLV seroprevalence in this specific shelter cat population was 15.3% (50/326). Out of these, 9.8% of the cats were FIV positive while 2.1% were FeLV positive and 3.4% were co-infected by both viruses. FIV infections were recorded exclusively in adult cats and thus a higher prevalence was detected among this age group compared with kittens, whereas gender, breed, coat length and municipality of origin were not associated with FIV or co-infection infection in the shelter population. These results show a relatively low FeLV prevalence and stable rates of the past two decades, contrasting with an elevated FIV prevalence recorded among shelter cats in Portugal compared with that described in other European countries. These findings suggest the urgent need for the implementation of a universal retroviral testing policy at shelter entrances, improvement of community vaccination initiatives, and enhanced viral surveillance measures in both feral and domestic cat populations in Portugal.
